summary:
The Russo-Japanese War of 1904–05 was a conflict between the Russian Empire and Imperial Japan over competing ambitions in Manchuria and Korea. Japan's decisive victory — the first defeat of a major European power by an Asian nation in the modern era — shocked the world, accelerated the decline of tsarist Russia, and established Japan as a major power capable of competing with the Western imperial nations on their own terms.

The war's origins lay in the competition for influence in the weakening Qing Empire's northeastern territories. Russia had used the Triple Intervention after the [[event:2550|First Sino-Japanese War]] to prevent Japan from retaining the Liaodong Peninsula, then proceeded to lease the same territory itself and use it to build a naval base at Port Arthur and expand its economic and military presence throughout Manchuria. Japan viewed this Russian expansion as a direct threat to its own Korean and Manchurian ambitions and to its national security.

Japan launched a surprise naval attack on the Russian fleet at Port Arthur on the night of 8–9 February 1904, before a formal declaration of war — a stratagem that Japan would repeat with far greater effect at Pearl Harbor 37 years later. The war that followed saw Japanese forces besiege and capture Port Arthur after months of costly fighting, defeat Russian relief armies at the Battle of Mukden in the largest land battle since Waterloo, and annihilate the Russian Baltic Fleet at the [[event:3442|Battle of Tsushima]] in May 1905 — a naval engagement of extraordinary completeness in which most of the Russian fleet was sunk or captured.

The Treaty of Portsmouth, mediated by American President Theodore Roosevelt (who won the Nobel Peace Prize for the effort), ended the war in September 1905. Russia acknowledged Japanese dominance in Korea, transferred the Liaodong lease and railroad rights in Manchuria to Japan, and ceded the southern half of Sakhalin Island. The war's domestic consequences in Russia were profound: the 1905 Revolution, triggered partly by the humiliation of defeat and the costs of war, forced Tsar Nicholas II to accept a constitution and an elected parliament, the Duma — though the reforms remained incomplete and the autocracy was not fundamentally transformed until 1917.
